mission.title: Install and Configure Strimzi on Kubernetes
mission.description: Strimzi provides a way to run an Apache Kafka® cluster on Kubernetes or OpenShift in various deployment configurations. Installing Strimzi allows you to easily manage Kafka clusters and their components within your Kubernetes environment.

steps.title: Add the Strimzi Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Strimzi Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration to access the Strimzi charts.
```bash
helm repo add strimzi https://strimzi.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Strimzi Kafka Operator
steps.description:
Install the Strimzi Kafka Operator in a dedicated namespace called 'kafka' using Helm.
```bash
helm install strimzi strimzi/strimzi-kafka-operator --namespace kafka --create-namespace --version 0.50.1
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Strimzi Kafka Operator pod is running successfully.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kafka
```

steps.title: Deploy a Kafka cluster
steps.description:
Create a Kafka cluster by applying the provided example Kafka resource configuration.
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://strimzi.io/examples/latest/kafka/kafka-persistent.yaml --namespace kafka
```

steps.title: Verify the Kafka cluster deployment
steps.description:
Ensure that the Kafka cluster pods are running properly.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kafka
```

steps.title: Post-install configuration
steps.description:
You may want to configure resource limits for your Kafka cluster. Edit the Kafka resource to set limits as needed.
```bash
kubectl edit kafka my-cluster --namespace kafka
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will have the Strimzi Kafka Operator running in the 'kafka' namespace, and you will see the Kafka cluster pods running without issues. You can further configure your Kafka cluster as needed.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add strimzi https://strimzi.io/charts
helm repo update

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description: Uninstall the Strimzi Kafka Operator release from the 'kafka' namespace using Helm.

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description: Remove the Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and any persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with the Kafka cluster.

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description: Delete the 'kafka' namespace and verify that it has been removed.

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description: Before upgrading, back up the current Kafka cluster configuration and any important data.

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description: Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Strimzi Kafka Operator to the latest version.

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description: Check that the Strimzi Kafka Operator and Kafka cluster are running correctly after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
This issue occurs when a pod fails to start repeatedly. Check the pod logs for errors using the following command:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace kafka
```

troubleshooting.title: Kafka cluster not responding
troubleshooting.description:
If the Kafka cluster is not responding, check the status of the Kafka pods:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace kafka
```

troubleshooting.title: Persistent volume claims not bound
troubleshooting.description:
If PVCs are not bound, check the status of the PVCs using:
```bash
kubectl get pvc --namespace kafka
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter an error stating that the Helm release is not found, verify the release name and namespace:
```bash
helm list --namespace kafka
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
